transcript:
[08:45] Sarah: You've missed three commitments in a row. The API was supposed to be live in January. It's March.
[09:15] Sarah: We built our entire Q1 onboarding workflow around that API. We had to do everything manually.
[09:48] Sarah: My team spent 40 hours last month doing work your product was supposed to automate.
[10:02] Sarah: We're actively evaluating Salesforce and HubSpot right now. Just so you're aware.
[10:31] Sarah: I don't know. Maybe. But I need to see action, not promises.
[11:04] Sarah: If you can get the API live this week and give me a written commitment on the next two milestones, I'll hold off the evaluation.
[11:52] Sarah: Okay. But I mean it — this is the last chance.
csm_notes: This was a difficult call. Sarah is legitimately frustrated — three API delays caused real operational pain. But she gave us a specific bar to clear (API this week + written commitment on next two milestones). She didn't say she's leaving — she said she's evaluating. There's still a path here if we move fast.
background: The API GA delays were caused by an internal infrastructure change that the customer was not properly informed about. Engineering has confirmed the API will be live by March 28.
competitor_context: Sarah mentioned Salesforce and HubSpot. Both are 6-12 months away from feature parity on the specific workflow she uses. Her evaluation threat is real but the alternatives are worse for her use case.
